messages: Is it acceptable to bring printed resumes or notes to an in-person interview, or does that feel unprepared?
If asked about leadership style, is it better to reference frameworks or to focus solely on specific decisions and outcomes?
How do I present trade-offs clearly when explaining why I chose one architecture over another?
Do you have a short checklist for the night before the interview to make sure tech and logistics are covered?

Strategically: given the session has distinct behavioral and technical portions and the intro needs to be very concise, should I spend more prep time on brief impact stories or on solving extra practice problems?
If I practice mock sessions with a friend, would a few rapid rehearsals of the opening plus focused coding drills be better than one long, uninterrupted mock?
They stressed keeping visuals compact — would it be safer to prepare a single tidy labeled sketch that fits on one page rather than a complex multi-part diagram?
For the opening line, I'm torn between memorizing a tight script and using bullet points; for a strictly timed opener, which approach tends to come across better?
If I find myself running long on a behavioral story early on, is it better to abbreviate it and promise a follow-up example later, or to finish even if it eats into other parts?
If an interviewer asks for more drawing than seems reasonable, should I agree and risk taking time, or keep visuals minimal and focus on verbal explanation?
There's an optional short pre-interview call that would shift the start earlier — with such a compact agenda, is extra pre-chat generally helpful or more likely to throw off my timing?
Between squeezing in one last long mock tonight and doing several short, focused practice drills over the next few days, which better mirrors what I should prioritize?
For pacing, if a story is getting long midway through the behavioral section, should I cut it short to stay on schedule, or complete it and accept less time later?
Would practicing diagram sketches that use labels only (no long explanations) be a reliable way to meet the interview's visual expectations?
When planning arrival or login time, how much of a buffer would you recommend so I'm mentally ready and not rushed at the start?

Final prioritization: given the limited opening window and the heavier technical portion, if I can add only one extra prep item this week, should it be perfecting one concise story or an additional technical drill?
